title: Making a simple drape mould
subtitle: Using bowls, plates, and domes as forms for drape-moulded clay dishes
excerpt: A drape mould is any convex surface a slab of clay is draped over to take its shape. Upturned salad bowls, dinner plates, and rounded rocks all work. No specialist equipment or kiln is needed. This tutorial covers selecting a drape mould, preparing a clay slab, draping it to shape, and releasing the clay without distortion.
sourceNotes: Drape-mould forming using convex surfaces is a universal hand-building technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A
drape mould
is any smooth convex surface that a clay slab can be shaped over. The clay takes the curve of the mould as it stiffens to
leather-hard

, then releases on its own as it shrinks slightly. No kiln or wheel is needed. An upturned salad bowl produces a shallow dish; a round-bottomed stainless mixing bowl produces a deeper one; a small upturned side plate makes a butter dish. The technique works with air-dry clay and paper clay.

attrs.body: The mould must be smooth so the clay releases cleanly. Ceramic and metal bowls work well; rough-surfaced plastic can tear the clay on release. A gentle curve releases more easily than a steep one. Avoid moulds that taper inward at the top: the clay will grip and crack on release.
attrs.title: Choosing a mould

content.text: What you need

items.name: Smooth upturned bowl or plate (the mould)
Thin cotton fabric or cling film to line the mould
Pottery rolling pin and guide rails

content.text: Preparing the mould

Place the bowl upside down on the work surface so the outside is facing up. Cover the outside surface with a thin piece of cotton fabric (a torn piece of old sheet or tea towel works well) or a single layer of cling film. The fabric or film acts as a release layer: without it, air-dry clay can bond to ceramic or metal as it dries.
Smooth the fabric firmly against the mould, pulling out any folds. A fold in the fabric will print as a ridge in the finished dish.
Rolling and draping the slab
Step 1: Roll a slab slightly larger than the mould

Roll a clay slab 6 to 8 mm thick and slightly larger in diameter than the widest point of the mould. A slab for a 25-cm bowl should be at least 28 cm across so the edges reach the rim. Use guide rails to keep the thickness even.
Step 2: Lift the slab onto the mould

Peel the slab from the rolling board by sliding a fettling knife underneath. Fold the slab in half loosely, carry it to the mould, and lay the fold over the centre of the mould. Unfold carefully and let the slab drape down the sides under its own weight. Smooth the centre first with a damp sponge, pressing gently outward to seat the slab against the mould surface.
Step 3: Smooth and trim the edge

Once the slab is draped flat against the mould, smooth the surface with a damp sponge to remove finger marks and compress the clay against the mould. Trim the edge with the fettling knife to a clean circle, cutting at the level you want the rim of the finished dish. A gentle wavy or irregular trim can be attractive if cut deliberately.
Step 4: Allow to reach leather-hard, then release

Leave the draped clay on the mould for two to four hours, uncovered, until the clay reaches leather-hard stage. At leather-hard it will have shrunk slightly away from the mould and will release cleanly when lifted from one edge. Do not force the release while the clay is still wet: it will distort. Lift the dish off the mould with both hands under the rim, then set it right-side-up on a flat board to finish drying.
What the
hump-mould
variation produces

The method above is a hump mould: clay draped over the outside of the mould. A slump mould, by contrast, places the clay inside a concave form, such as inside an upturned bowl placed the right way up. Slump moulds produce a slightly different profile: the outside of the finished dish is the surface that was pressed against the mould. Hump moulds produce a dish with an interior that matches the mould's exterior curvature.
Common problems

attrs.intro: Problems with drape moulding and how to address them.

items.fix: Always use fabric or cling film. If stuck, wait longer: the clay will release naturally as it dries and shrinks further.
items.cause: No release layer was used, or the clay was removed too wet.
items.symptom: Dish sticks to the mould and tears on release

items.fix: Fold the slab precisely in half before placing on the mould to find the centre. Use the mould edge as a guide when trimming.
items.cause: The slab was not centred on the mould before draping, or the trim was freehand.
items.symptom: Rim is uneven around the dish

items.fix: Cover the rim loosely with a strip of plastic for the first few hours of drying to slow the rim and allow the base to catch up.
items.cause: The rim dried faster than the base, which is the thinnest part.
items.symptom: Cracks develop at the rim as the dish dries

attrs.heading: Common problems
